data.title: Interview with Zeng Xinxun, founder of Liangpei: After Kimi left, he started working as an AI matchmaker
data.description:
During WAIC, Zeng Xinxun's product did not appear on any of the booths. His only public introduction of "Liangpei" at the event was during a flash debate organized by the WeChat Mini Program team. The topic was: Should AI-powered relationship products allow users to "use and leave," or should they pursue "long-term stickiness"? Zeng Xinxun chose the former. In an industry where all products try their best to retain users, he believes that the true success of dating apps is for users to find the right person quickly and then leave. AI should not be a substitute for a long-term relationship; it should step back after the match is complete. A year ago, Zeng Xinxun left Kimi to start this AI dating product called "Liangpei". Before that, he worked on search and recommendation at WeChat and TikTok, and his last job was as the head of AI search technology at Kimi. Search solves the problem of matching information with needs. Now, he wants to replace documents with people. To enter the matching system, users first need to have a 20-minute voice conversation with the AI, allowing the model to understand their personality, experiences, and mate selection criteria. The AI ​​then compares the profiles of two people and analyzes the probability of mutual compatibility. After entering the communication phase, it can also act as an "avatar" and "strategist," answering awkward questions, reading chat history, and helping people determine the progress of a relationship. However, the truly unique aspect of this product isn't entirely in the AI. Liangpei requires users to fill out their profiles carefully, allows matching with only one person at a time, doesn't restrict the exchange of WeChat contacts, and even offers a "money-back guarantee if you don't get married within three years" membership program. Almost every design element actively abandons the registration conversion, activity, and retention metrics valued by traditional internet products. Why would someone who used to work in search want to use AI to handle intimate relationships? What should AI do for humans, and where should it stop? How does a product that wants users to leave as quickly as possible ultimately plan to monetize? We talked to Zeng Xinxun. Products grow from problems. This isn't Zeng Xinxun's first entrepreneurial venture. More than ten years ago, he was a student at Southern University of Science and Technology. At that time, food delivery platforms had just emerged, and he and his classmates started an online restaurant called "Nanfeng Diandian," delivering cooked food to university dormitories. This story also stems from his own life. He's a heavy user of food delivery services. In an era when you still needed to flip through menus, make phone calls, and repeatedly explain your address, he felt ordering a meal shouldn't be so complicated. So he restructured the process of offline restaurants and tried to complete ordering, preparation, and delivery online. More than ten years later, he entered the dating and marriage market, which already had a large number of products and had existed for more than twenty years, starting from his own life again. First encounter a problem, then create a product. This is the entrepreneurial approach that Zeng Xinxun is most familiar with. Zeng Xinxun's first entrepreneurial venture targeted the university market, focusing on delivery to the bed, and orders would surge on rainy days. Beating: When you first started your campus food delivery business, there weren't any particularly dominant platforms in the market. Now, you're in the dating industry, facing a market that's been around for over twenty years and has a plethora of products. What are the similarities between these two ventures? Zeng Xinxun: All my product ideas actually come from my own life. I started a food delivery business because I'm a super heavy user of food delivery services. I probably order over a hundred times a year. Back then, ordering food delivery involved spending half a day communicating over the phone, and I felt this process was too complicated. There should be a more reasonable, more digital solution, so I started doing it. At the time, we were operating in several universities in Guangdong, accumulating a considerable user base. Initially, I didn't think about platforms, competition, or the future market landscape. My competitor wasn't another food delivery platform, but the old experience of ordering food by phone. Later, large platforms like Meituan entered the market, raising funds and subsidies, burning through hundreds of millions or even billions of yuan, which is how we were driven out. That's a story for later. The same goes for Liangpei; its inspiration came from my own blind date experiences. The difference is that dating is a sector with over twenty years of history, with platforms like Zhenai.com and Jiayuan.com, and later, a series of dating products such as Soul, Momo, and Tantan. I myself was a heavy user of these products, and I felt they didn't truly solve my problems. With the advent of AI, I believe this experience can be completely transformed. Beating: What was your experience finding a partner like? Zeng Xinxun: I took a three-year break from school when I started my first business, so I was already 25 when I graduated with my bachelor's degree. Looking back, I realized that from 18 to 25, I was either starting a business, working, or preparing for graduation, and I hadn't had a serious relationship. I felt it was a real pity. If 20 to 30 is a precious period in life, half of that time had already passed, and I was still single. So after graduation, I really made "finding a partner" my KPI. At first, I looked around, seeing if there were any suitable people among my colleagues and classmates. After searching around, I found that most of the people I admired already had partners. Then I started participating in various activities. I participated in company-organized hikes, mountain climbing, badminton, frisbee, werewolf games, and murder mystery games—any opportunity to meet new people. I also attended social events organized by the school alumni association and posted on the company's internal dating forum. Having tried offline methods without success, I registered on a dating app. I spent over a year on it but still didn't meet the right person. Finally, I considered giving up and uninstalled the app. A few months later, it was New Year's Day again. I realized another year had passed, and I still hadn't achieved my goal, so I felt a little unwilling and reinstalled the app. After logging back in, I saw that I had only received one message in the past few months—from my current wife. We chatted online for a day, met the next day, and got together that same day. Now, six years have passed, and we have a one-year-old daughter. This process made me realize that finding a truly suitable partner today is not an easy task. Whether you look within your immediate circle, expand your network through events, or use an online platform that seems to offer many options, there are many difficulties. These difficulties were difficult for technology to handle in the past because interpersonal relationships are so non-standardized. I think the fuzzy logic of large models has, for the first time, the opportunity to address some of these issues. A Luxurious Two Thousand Words When using dating apps, Zeng Xinxun wrote himself a personal introduction of over two thousand words. It included his personality, family, education and entrepreneurial experiences, as well as future plans, lifestyle habits, diet, and daily routine. He wanted to present himself as completely as possible. If there was anything the other person couldn't accept, they could eliminate him as early as possible. After writing it, he received fewer "likes." But it was these two thousand words that ultimately led him to the right person. One of his wife's classmates saw his profile first and forwarded it to the research group group. She registered for the software because of this profile and sent him a message. However, Zeng Xinxun had already uninstalled the app by then and only saw it several months later. Both of them wrote very long descriptions of themselves. Zeng Xinxun wrote over two thousand words, and his wife wrote over one thousand. They didn't meet because they had too many choices; precisely because they were both willing to seriously reveal themselves. Beating: What do you think was the core problem with past dating or matchmaking apps? Zeng Xinxun: The first problem was that users provided too little information. On many past dating apps, besides a few photos and some tags, it was difficult to truly understand a person. This lack of information stemmed from two issues: firstly, a lack of willingness—many people didn't have high expectations for dating apps and were unwilling to invest; and secondly, a lack of ability—not everyone could logically and systematically introduce themselves, and many people probably didn't know themselves very well. So, you could only gradually increase your understanding through chatting and meeting in person. This process required three things. The first was time. You might spend a weekend afternoon meeting someone. Secondly, there's the cost—food, taxis—all have expenses. Thirdly, and most importantly, there's the mental and emotional investment. You need to enthusiastically introduce yourself to a stranger, patiently listen to them, and show interest in their life. But ultimately, you're likely not a good match, and all that investment becomes a sunk cost. My patience, time, and emotions should be reserved for those who deserve more. There's no need for everyone to go through the entire process of trial and error on their own. AI can at least help provide more context and then filter out obviously unsuitable people before the meeting even begins.

Liangpei's AI Voice Interaction Page

Beating: But getting users to fill in more information doesn't just happen naturally with AI. How exactly does Liangpei improve the richness of the information?

Zeng Xinxun:We turned filling in the information into an AI-guided voice dialogue.

Not everyone can write a short essay, but if someone talks to you for 20 minutes, most people can share a fairly good picture of their situation, provided they're willing to cooperate. What kind of person are you? Do you prefer stability or adventure? Do you want to continue building your career or lead a relatively peaceful life? What are your views on marriage, children, and cities? These can all be gradually developed during the conversation. We've internally analyzed some competitors, such as Qing Teng Zhi Lian and Qian Shou, and their average user profiles are only about 100 words long. Liangpei's current user profiles average over 400 words. When a person is no longer just a few photos and tags, but becomes a more three-dimensional individual, both parties have a basis for judging whether they are compatible. Beating: When people introduce themselves, they often subconsciously embellish their self-image. How can AI distinguish between a person's self-image and their true self? Zeng Xinxun: We can't completely distinguish them, and I don't think it's our obligation. In normal interpersonal interactions, everyone embellishes themselves slightly. When you see someone's self-description, you'll naturally discount it a bit. Someone might say they're 1.7 meters tall, but they might be 1.68 meters; someone might say they're 1.8 meters tall, but they might be 1.75 meters. But you need at least an original price before deciding how much to discount. What we do is help people give a more complete and comprehensive "original price." As for the true situation, it still needs to be confirmed step by step through subsequent interactions. This is better than having no information at all and having to guess from scratch. Beating: With more data, why can't traditional products still solve the matching problem? Zeng Xinxun: Even with a lot of data, past products might not be able to understand it. I wrote two thousand words, but the platform didn't recommend me to people who might actually appreciate that content. Was my wife the only person in Shenzhen who might like me? Definitely not. The reason is that for traditional recommendation algorithms, two thousand words might just be a piece of text of two thousand characters. They can extract keywords, tag it, and then perform tag matching, but it's difficult to understand the underlying meaning. For example, I've written about my entrepreneurial experience, which reflects that I might enjoy taking risks, but it could also mean that my life isn't very stable. Some people might see this and think it's unsuitable, while others might like it. I once met a girl introduced to me by a teacher. We chatted on WeChat for a week, got along very well, and even became a little flirtatious. But after our first meeting, after I told her about my entrepreneurial experience, she went home and told me to forget it. She wanted a more stable life and didn't want to live in constant fear, worrying about losing her job or her business failing. But I've also met people who are completely the opposite. She doesn't like working in a company all the time and instead hopes her partner is willing to take risks. The same characteristic can be a disadvantage for some people and an advantage for others. If technology cannot understand this difference, it can only continuously orchestrate ineffective communication. When I worked on AI search at Kimi, the essence was to first understand the user's needs, then understand the document, and determine if the two could match. Now, it's simply replacing that document with another person. AI doesn't date people. AI's role in romantic relationships is delicate. If a product aims for long chat sessions, it's better suited to be a companion; if it aims for matching efficiency, it's more like a middleman; if it aims to provide more choices, it will constantly bring new people to the user; if it considers truly establishing a relationship as success, it must also know when to exit. The same technology, because of different service metrics, will ultimately serve completely different people. Zeng Xinxun defines the boundaries of AI as follows: it can understand, filter, relay, and remind, but it cannot replace a person in liking another person. AI handles the parts that don't require human intervention, leaving time, effort, and emotion for the parts that truly require human involvement.

Beating: In your opinion, which parts of a relationship should AI intervene in, and where should it stop?

Zeng Xinxun:AI is meant to assist people, not make decisions for them. It can't designate a person and tell them: You're a good match, so be together. That's ridiculous.

Beating: In your opinion, which parts of a relationship should AI intervene in, and where should it stop?

Zeng Xinxun:AI is meant to assist people, not make decisions for them. It can't just designate a person and tell them: You're a good match, so be together.

... A chemical reaction is needed between people. You need to meet, spend time together, and invest time to build a relationship. These can only be done by humans. However, not all the work in this process has to be done by humans. First, AI can help you explain the information clearly. Second, it can understand this information, perform searches, and make recommendations. Third, it can also act as a mediator during communication between two people. For example, we've set up an AI avatar in Liangpei (a matchmaking platform). Each user has their own avatar, and others can ask it questions. Some questions are repetitive, such as which city they plan to develop in the future, or whether they have marriage plans; some questions are more awkward, such as past romantic experiences, family situation, home purchase plans, whether parents have retirement funds, or views on bride price. If the answer is already in the user's profile, the AI ​​avatar can answer directly. If not, it will anonymously relay the question to the user to solicit an answer, and will adjust the wording, removing any expressions that might make the user uncomfortable. This both supplements a person's profile and avoids everyone repeatedly answering the same questions like customer service representatives. Beating: Besides answering questions for people, how does the AI ​​strategist intervene in conversations between two people? Zeng Xinxun: The AI ​​strategist will read both parties' profiles and chat history to provide suggestions to users. We have seen a pair of users... The boy and girl both asked the AI ​​the same question: "Based on our chat history, does the other person have feelings for me?" They both wanted to move the relationship forward, but neither was sure. In the past, you might have asked a friend or best friend, but that required explaining a lot of context, and even friends didn't know the other person well. The AI ​​advisor provided a relatively complete perspective in this scenario. We later planned to add a feature. If both parties asked similar questions, a hidden Easter egg would appear, called "Unanimous" or "Telepathic Connection," telling them: You're both actually very curious about each other now. What people really fear is being the only one invested, the fear of becoming the clown. Few people would mind a confirmed mutual attraction. Another girl was chatting very well with a guy, but on the third day, he suddenly stopped replying to her messages. She sent messages in the morning, noon, and evening, and then asked the AI: Why isn't he replying to me? The AI ​​initially told her that he was pursuing a PhD and might be busy, and that she could wait a little longer. The next day, she asked again how much longer she had to wait. The AI ​​told her she could wait a maximum of three days. If there was still no response after three days, it wasn't just that he was busy; it could also mean he wasn't interested in continuing the relationship. On the third day, she asked if she should unmatch the guy. The AI ​​suggested she end it, and she did. Later, when we followed up by phone, she said she really liked the guy's profile and was reluctant to give up easily. However, being in the thick of it makes it difficult to make decisions for oneself. AI gave her an outlet to review and exit. Beating: Aren't you worried that users will leave Liangpei after adding each other's contact information? Zeng Xinxun: Let them leave. Our goal is to solve users' needs for finding partners. If you exchange contact information, it means your relationship has progressed to the point where you need to switch platforms to chat, and we've already accomplished part of our mission. Why would I keep you around? Initially, some users were very cautious when exchanging contact information. They would send a WeChat QR code and then withdraw it, or send their phone number in three parts, perhaps because they were used to other platforms restricting referrals. Later, we added a prompt. When the system detects that a user has sent their WeChat ID or phone number, it will directly tell them that the platform does not restrict the exchange of contact information. It's a good thing that users have entered into a real relationship. Most internet products try to lower the registration threshold. Information can be filled in later, identity can be verified later; get users in first, then figure out how to improve activity and retention. Liangpei chose the opposite direction. Users need to complete approximately 20 minutes of AI dialogue and meet a certain standard of detail in their profile before they can officially enter the product. It also includes real-name authentication, unmarried status verification, and real-person profile picture verification. These steps will lower the registration conversion rate and cause some people who just want to browse to leave early. This is precisely the purpose of the design. Zeng Xinxun doesn't intend to persuade everyone to stay. He first determines what kind of people deserve to be served, and then keeps those who don't belong to this stage out.

Zeng Xinxun attends alumni event

Beating: What kind of users are you looking for?

Zeng Xinxun:Currently, we mainly target urban white-collar workers. This is because users need to express themselves clearly and be able to articulate their needs. This is related to age, education, and life experience.

But the most important requirement is only one: they genuinely want to find a partner. People's attitudes towards relationships differ at different stages of life. Sometimes it's just casual browsing, sometimes they're eager to get married, and sometimes they're preparing to seriously look for a partner. "Good Match" is only suitable for the last type of person. The first 20 minutes of conversation and profile review are designed to allow users who don't currently belong to this group to naturally leave. This way, those who remain on the platform at least know that both parties' expectations are aligned. Everyone is willing to seriously pursue a relationship with the goal of a long-term commitment, even marriage. Beating: Why does "Good Match" have a one-to-one matching system? Before confirming whether someone is suitable, users usually want to compare several options. Zeng Xinxun: Other platforms generally allow chatting with many people simultaneously. Users send "likes" to many people, and after the others reply, many chat partners appear in the list. They chat a few words here and a few words there, and in the end, they might not be able to chat with anyone. The mechanism of Liangpei is that you can send "likes" to different people, but as long as one person responds, the two enter a one-to-one matching state. At this time, neither of them will see other recommendations or receive new messages from others. They will only return to the recommendation list when one party unmatches them. AI can do the initial screening, but after finding a potentially suitable person, you need to invest time and focus to develop a relationship. In the past, no one believed they would be taken seriously. You didn't know if you were the tenth person on someone's list. We hope that AI and humans can form a division of labor. AI will do the initial screening and understanding work, while humans will do the later part that requires emotional investment. The premise of one-on-one is that you can't treat someone as a backup option. Beating: Won't this mechanism create pressure for people? Unmatching feels like directly rejecting someone. Zeng Xinxun: This is indeed a common user feedback issue. Many people rarely say no directly, so they feel psychological pressure when ending a relationship. But if you keep dozens of uncertain chat contacts because of pressure to refuse, the end result might be that no one gets taken seriously. My investor initially opposed the one-on-one model. She said that as an investor, she needs to review many projects and compare them repeatedly before investing in one. Why can't you look at other people after reviewing one when looking for a partner? My answer was that investors are in a relatively dominant position when reviewing projects; they are making the decisions. But in close relationships, both parties are equal. While you are choosing others, others are also choosing you. If everyone wants to keep more choices at the same time, they may end up in a common predicament. Everyone has many people on their list, but no one is willing to commit first. We talked about this issue for a long time and also conducted some user interviews. Finally, she accepted that one-on-one might be a very differentiated mechanism for finding a good match, and also a way to improve matching results. Beating: Will you extend this technology to finding friends, partners, or professional networking in the future? Zeng Xinxun: At least not in the Beating product. A product needs to solve one problem first before it can be continuously deepened around that problem. Some AI social products nowadays want to help users find friends, partners, lovers, and jobs. They try to do everything, but in the end, each relationship can only be very superficial. Because if any function is made too deep, it will feel redundant to users with other purposes. Liangpei prioritizes mastering the core of dating and matchmaking. This technology might be applied to other products later, but it won't cram all relationships into a single product. "Don't believe in user retention" isn't a new concept for dating products. Hinge, a dating app, has long defined itself as an app "designed to be deleted," and established Hinge Labs, staffed by behavioral researchers, to study what profiles, matches, and interactions are more likely to lead users to real dates. Liangpei takes this a step further. It not only doesn't equate retention with success, but also attempts to link revenue to the final outcome. Users who purchase the "Marriage Guarantee Membership Plan" can apply for a full refund if they don't register their marriage within three years. In Zeng Xinxun's design, this is a business model derived from product values. The platform cannot simultaneously hope for user success while relying on continued user engagement to generate revenue. However, just because values ​​can derive product mechanisms doesn't mean it's inherently a viable business. Beating: Why was "marriage" ultimately defined as product delivery, rather than matching, meeting, or establishing a romantic relationship? Zeng Xinxun: This model was derived step by step. We use AI, which consumes tokens, and we've invested a lot of resources to help users. The goal should be to make it easier for them to find a partner, not to make them stay on the platform longer. Given this, our business model cannot be solely linked to user time spent on the platform. Otherwise, we'll be contradicting ourselves: wanting you to succeed on one hand, but not wanting you to succeed on the other. Therefore, revenue should be related to results. But what constitutes success needs further definition. In the first version, we considered it success if users exchanged contact information and met offline. This step was very easy to verify; we just needed to see if both parties exchanged WeChat contacts. But users didn't accept this. They would say, "I only added them on WeChat, we haven't met in person yet"; or "We met and parted on bad terms, how can that be considered success?" Let's continue. Establishing a romantic relationship is generally considered a success. However, it's difficult to verify. Anyone can claim they haven't been in a relationship, making it impossible for the platform to confirm. Next comes marriage. Marriage is a mutually agreed-upon milestone and can be verified through marriage registration. Therefore, the final solution becomes marriage as the delivery. Regarding the timeframe, we conducted a small-sample survey of over a hundred registered marriage applicants. According to our survey results, if two people met after graduation and while working, rather than developing a relationship from classmates, most would decide whether to marry within three years of meeting. This led to the "money-back guarantee if you don't marry within three years." We hope the entire product and success rate form a closed loop. By improving matching and communication effectiveness, users are more likely to succeed, allowing the platform to generate revenue. Beating: Your team currently has 17 people, but none have a background in psychology or intimate relationship research. Relying solely on models and product experience, how do you ensure the matching logic is reliable enough? Xinxun Zeng: We don't have any yet, but we definitely will in the future. After Hinge reached a certain scale, we also established our own research team specifically to study intimate relationships and user behavior within the product. University psychology researchers have many theories, but the platform has firsthand data. How users meet, how they communicate, and how relationships develop—only the product team can continuously access this data. In the future, we also hope to establish a similar team to study how relationships are formed on the platform, and then use these results to further optimize the model. Our goal is for Liangpei to ultimately become one of the models that best understands intimate relationships. Products should have their own judgment. Zeng Xinxun roughly divides product managers into two categories. One type believes more in experience, intuition, and value judgment. The other type relies more on data, experimentation, and reusable methods. He also maps these two tendencies to his own work experience. At WeChat, he felt that products must first have stable values; at TikTok and ByteDance, he felt a lot of A/B testing and rapid iteration, leaving different solutions to data to judge. Liangpei is closer to the former. Many of its choices may not perform best in short-term data. A 20-minute voice call might lower registration rates, one-on-one interaction might reduce engagement, allowing WeChat exchanges might lower retention, and a refund policy for non-marriage might slow revenue growth. These choices depend on the founders making initial judgments and then using the product to validate those judgments.

2019-2022, Zeng Xinxun worked in Tencent's WeChat search team

Beating: You divide product managers into "experience-oriented" and "method-oriented" types, and you also apply this to your work experience at WeChat and ByteDance. What are the differences between these two approaches?

Beating: You divide product managers into "experience-oriented" and "method-oriented" types, and you also apply this to your work experience at WeChat and ByteDance. What are the differences between these two approaches?

Still from the TV show "Hang the DJ"

Image source: Douban

The position that Zeng Xinxun arranged for Liangpei is similar to this.

AI first helps users understand themselves, filters out obviously unsuitable people, and then puts two potentially suitable people in front of each other.

AI first helps users understand themselves, filters out obviously unsuitable people, and then puts two potentially suitable people in front of each other.

Once users start investing time, building rapport, and even switching to WeChat to continue chatting, the product should take a backseat. In this logic, user departure isn't a loss, but a success. This product logic is complete, but the business logic isn't fully established. If a user doesn't marry within three years, the membership fee needs to be refunded; during this period, the platform still bears the costs of tokens, authentication, services, operations, and customer acquisition. Marriage is influenced by family, city, economy, personality, and personal choices, making it difficult to attribute entirely to a single algorithmic match. A more realistic issue is that "Good Match" still needs to rapidly expand its user base. Dating matching heavily relies on user density within the same city, of similar age, and with similar needs. The 20-minute voice chat, one-on-one matching, and stricter verification thresholds will actively filter out some people. These designs can filter out those who don't take relationships seriously, but they may also make it harder for a product that already needs scale to achieve it. How to price memberships, how to manage funds, what revenue will cover costs over three years, and how the platform verifies whether a marriage is facilitated by a good match—these questions remain unanswered. Currently, "money-back guarantee if you don't get married" is more like a strong product promise than a proven business model. In "Hang the DJ," the match only truly works when two people leave the system. Liangpei also hopes users will leave in this way. However, it first needs to prove that it can stay until that day.

data.title: The suspense surrounding the implementation of Russia's new law on A7 and A5 has only just begun.
data.description: Russia has just enshrined the provision allowing foreign trade participants to use digital currencies for settlement in cross-border transactions into the main text of its law. However, in reality, a ruble-based stablecoin has been on this path for over a year—it's called A7A5. The new law aims to establish a compliant channel under the guise of the state; A7A5, on the other hand, is taking a completely different route. In February 2025, Old Vector, a company registered in Kyrgyzstan, minted its first batch of A7A5 stablecoins, pegged to the ruble, with reserve assets held in the core of the Russian defense system—Promsviaz Bank (PSB), which has been sanctioned by the West for many years. Beyond the issuing entity, the A7 network to which this stablecoin belongs has been identified by the US Treasury Department as being linked to Moldovan oligarch Ilan Shore. Shore himself was sentenced in absentia for a billion-dollar bank fraud case and faces sanctions from the US, UK, and other countries. According to reports, by January 2026, A7A5's cumulative on-chain transaction volume exceeded $100 billion, becoming one of the world's largest non-USD stablecoins in less than a year, with approximately 250,000 transactions and over 41,000 accounts. This origin destined it to be no ordinary stablecoin project from day one. The most counterintuitive aspect of A7A5's history is its reaction to sanctions—not shrinking, but expanding. In August 2025, the U.S. Treasury Department took action against Grinex, the main exchange for A7A5. However, contrary to expectations, the trading volume of this stablecoin did not shrink; instead, its market capitalization rose, briefly approaching $500 million, reaching more than 40% of the total market capitalization of non-USD stablecoins at the time. Reports citing on-chain data suggest that a significant proportion of tokens in the A7A5 network underwent destruction and reminting in new wallets after the associated exchanges were sanctioned. This method objectively severs the connection between frozen addresses and new tokens. However, it should be noted that the specific proportion and triggering timing currently lack cross-verification from official reports from first-hand on-chain analysis institutions such as Elliptic and TRM Labs. It remains a detail worth observing but has not yet been confirmed. Regardless of whether these specific operational details can ultimately be confirmed, a simpler fact suffices to illustrate the point: despite Garantex's shutdown and Grinex's sanctions, this coin didn't die; instead, it grew stronger. This at least demonstrates that relying on freezing a few exchanges to shut down a crypto settlement channel is far less effective than imagined. A7A5's survival to this day isn't solely due to these countermeasures; it also relies on a sophisticated legal framework. Russia has a Digital Financial Assets Law (259-FZ) from 2021 that allows licensed "information system operators" to issue regulated digital financial assets (DFAs), which must be linked to private blockchains and issued by licensed institutions like Sberbank and Alfa-Bank—sounds completely different from public blockchain stablecoins like A7A5. However, according to an analysis by the Harvard Davis Center, in October 2025, the Central Bank of Russia issued a "letter of no objection," which some research institutions interpreted as tacit approval by Russian regulators for the cross-border use of A7A5. It's important to note, however, that this is still the analytical wording used by research institutions regarding the Central Bank of Russia's actions, not an official characterization publicly used by the Central Bank of Russia itself. The official status of A7A5 is actually "Foreign-issued Digital Rights" (FDRs), which doesn't entirely align with the original design intent of 259-FZ, but regulatory agencies tacitly approved it. This is the path A7A5 has taken over the past two years: no specific law was tailored for it, but rather an ambiguous document from the central bank opened the door to cross-border settlements without prior consultation. The new Duma law essentially paved the way into a legitimate avenue—allowing the use of digital currency in foreign trade contract settlements is directly written into the law, eliminating the need for an ambiguous letter from the central bank. However, the new law establishes a domestic registration list: exchanges, clearinghouses, and brokers must all be registered with the Central Bank of Russia, and banks and financial institutions will face stricter compliance requirements. A7A5, on the other hand, is the opposite—its issuer is in Kyrgyzstan, but its trading venues are overseas exchanges and public blockchain DeFi protocols. Its cross-border structure objectively reduces the impact of a freeze in a single jurisdiction. This is the paradox the new law presents to A7A5: to reap the benefits of compliance, it needs to be registered domestically; but once a clear trace of its institutions and funds is left, its prized moat of "easily rebuildable and difficult to freeze" weakens. The new law seeks a visible and controllable national channel; the value of A7A5 lies precisely in its unseen and uncontrollable nature. Whether the two can be compatible is itself a question. After the ceasefire, who will be eliminated first? What truly worries A7A5 is not the sanctions themselves, but the possibility that they might end. As rumors of a Russia-Ukraine ceasefire intensify, Oleg Ogenko, a senior executive at A7A5, publicly stated that even if sanctions are lifted, the coin still has a reason to survive—faster and more convenient cross-border settlements. This sounds confident, but from another perspective, it also reveals unease: once the sanctions that underpin its existence disappear, what will it use to compete with the liquidity-dominant dollar-denominated stablecoins? The new Duma law, however, is not truly preparing for another stablecoin, but rather a national-level system for cross-border settlement of digital assets. In the past, Russia has relied more on market-based solutions like A7A5 in practice; in the future, whether it's a new compliant stablecoin, a regulated digital financial asset, or a digital ruble, they could all potentially play different roles within this framework, rather than relying on a single token already embroiled in sanctions for cross-border settlement capabilities. Even more coincidentally, the Central Bank of Russia also plans to further promote the wider application of the digital ruble. The almost simultaneous advancement of the institutional framework and the central bank digital currency signifies that Russia is attempting to gradually shift from a "shadow settlement network" that previously relied on spontaneous market formation to a state-led, regulated digital settlement system. A shadow token that survived through repeated maneuvering, a national law that has just gained legal status but requires strict documentation, and a digital ruble issued directly by the central bank—three lines are running simultaneously. What sanctions truly force out is never a single ingenious design, but rather a whole set of mutually reinforcing survival systems. Who will be eliminated first? And who will survive until the armistice?

data.title: Coinbase Opens New Singapore Office and Plans Major Hiring Drive Across Engineering and Institutional Sales
data.description:
Singapore Becomes Coinbase's Next Big Bet In Asia

Coinbase is putting fresh weight behind its Singapore business, with plans to lift its local headcount from around 150 to close to 200 staff by the end of 2026.

The Nasdaq-listed crypto exchange marked the milestone by opening a new office at One Raffles Quay on Wednesday, a move that country director Hassan Ahmed says reflects how central the city-state has become to the company's Asia-Pacific ambitions.

A new chapter begins.

Yesterday, we officially opened the doors to our permanent Singapore office.

Honoured to celebrate with Mr Kenneth Gay, Chief FinTech Officer of MAS, and Ms Seah Yueh Chinn, VP of Digital Industry Singapore at @singapore_edb — alongside friends from… pic.twitter.com/JKsDdRBQVI

— Coinbase Singapore 🛡️ (@CoinbaseSG) July 23, 2026

Which Teams Are Set To Grow

Speaking to The Business Times, Ahmed said the bulk of new roles will sit in engineering, customer service, relationship management and institutional sales.

The hiring push is notable given it runs opposite to Coinbase's global trajectory.

On 5 May 2026, the company cut roughly 14 per cent of its worldwide workforce, pointing to cost discipline in unpredictable markets and the growing role of artificial intelligence in its operations.

Management layers were also trimmed as part of that restructuring, with remaining staff redirected towards AI-focused work.

Why Is Coinbase Doubling Down On Singapore

Ahmed called Singapore "one of the world's most trusted financial hubs and one of Coinbase's fastest-growing international markets."

On the new office, he said:

"This new office reflects our long-term confidence in Singapore as a strategic hub for innovation, talent, and responsible growth across the Asia-Pacific, giving us the resources to work more closely with local authorities, invest in talent and scale partnerships."

He credited the country's early and clear rules for digital assets as a key draw, noting:

"It was much ahead of other jurisdictions and hubs that were also vying to be digital asset hubs at that time."

A supportive business climate, favourable tax policies and steady capital inflows were cited as additional reasons the exchange continues to invest there.

What's Driving Demand For Crypto In The Region

Ahmed pointed to two client groups fuelling growth, accredited investors and institutional players, both of which he said have sharpened their attention on digital assets and blockchain technology more broadly.

He described a "huge swell of interest" building around tokenisation as regulators worldwide take a more active role in shaping the space, citing the Genius Act in the United States, the European Union's Markets in Crypto-Assets framework and Hong Kong's stablecoin ordinance as examples.

Locally, the Monetary Authority of Singapore has introduced a tokenised bills pilot alongside its stablecoin regulatory framework.

Singapore plans to pilot tokenized MAS Bills settled with a wholesale CBDC in 2026. pic.twitter.com/jINEbvzfqK

— Coinsauce (@CoinsauceCrypto) November 13, 2025

On shifting user expectations, Ahmed said:

"Consumers and traders now have an expectation of 24/7 trading markets, and they want to apply this technology to tokenise assets to make them 24/7. They also want to use stablecoins to do instant settlement."

How Is Coinbase Positioned In Singapore Already

Coinbase secured its full major payment institution licence from the Monetary Authority of Singapore in October 2023, a step that opened the door to serving both individuals and institutions in the country.

Since then, it has backed the Singapore dollar-pegged stablecoin XSGD alongside StraitsX, and rolled out Coinbase Business to give companies access to stablecoin payment infrastructure.

Singapore's digital payment token licensing regime currently lists 38 approved entities, according to the MAS directory, after the regulator tightened requirements in 2025 and pushed unlicensed operators out of the market.

What Comes Next For AI And Digital Assets

Looking further ahead, Coinbase is examining how artificial intelligence and crypto could intersect, including giving AI agents their own stablecoin wallets so their blockchain activity can be tracked and audited.

AI is also being woven into internal engineering processes, though Ahmed stressed that human oversight will remain essential.

He said,

"Digital assets and AI are effectively colliding. We are very excited about the potential of AI."

How Coinbase's Stock Has Performed

The Singapore expansion arrives against a difficult backdrop for Coinbase shares, which have fallen 59 per cent over the past 12 months to trade at US$162.13, roughly S$208 based on current exchange rates.

data.title: Is Alex Atallah's "high-profile exit" scenario repeating itself?
data.description:
Author: Nancy, PANews

More than four years ago, Alex Atallah exited the NFT market at its peak; now, he is enjoying a high-profile moment in the AI ​​boom and is preparing to sell his AI model aggregation platform, OpenRouter, for a high price.

On July 23, according to the Wall Street Journal, payment giant Stripe is in talks to acquire OpenRouter, with a potential valuation close to $10 billion. If the deal goes through, it will be Alex Atallah's second successful attempt to build a multi-billion dollar company after his NFT platform, OpenSea. ...>

Stripe is reportedly planning to acquire OpenRouter, with a potential valuation of up to $10 billion. Rumors of OpenRouter being sold have been circulating for some time. Last week, several foreign media outlets, including The Information and Jawl, reported that OpenRouter had received acquisition offers from several large technology companies and was in discussions regarding a sale, with a potential transaction size of several billion dollars. According to the latest news from the Wall Street Journal, Stripe is considering acquiring the world's largest AI model aggregation platform. Sources familiar with the matter revealed that negotiations between the two parties may be announced soon, but the deal is still subject to change, and the possibility of negotiations breaking down or other potential buyers joining the bidding cannot be ruled out. Interestingly, Alex Atallah once described OpenRouter as "the Stripe of the AI ​​world." In his view, just as Stripe helps customers process various payments through a unified portal, OpenRouter aims to become a unified portal for enterprises to access different AI models, reducing the cost of switching between multiple AI vendors and avoiding being "locked in" by a single model vendor. In fact, OpenRouter and Stripe have already established a partnership. This potential transaction is also seen as an important step for the payment infrastructure giant to further extend into the AI ​​infrastructure field. It's worth noting that this could be another major acquisition for Stripe recently, following reports of its planned acquisition of PayPal. Currently, neither party has disclosed the specific transaction amount. However, according to sources, if the deal is finalized, OpenRouter's valuation at the time of sale could approach $10 billion. This figure will far exceed OpenRouter's valuation during its previous funding rounds. Founded just over three years ago, this AI infrastructure company has achieved rapid growth driven by the large-scale model wave. Public data shows that OpenRouter has completed three rounds of financing, raising over $150 million in total. In June 2025, OpenRouter announced the completion of a $40 million seed and Series A funding round, valuing the company at approximately $547 million post-money. On March 26, 2026, OpenRouter announced the completion of a $113 million Series B funding round, valuing the company at approximately $1.3 billion post-money. If this transaction ultimately goes through, it means that OpenRouter's valuation could increase nearly tenfold within months, joining the ranks of billion-dollar unicorns. The soaring value of OpenRouter stems not only from the explosive demand driven by the rapid expansion of its large-scale models but also from the increasing attention the AI ​​infrastructure sector is receiving from the capital market. Will this be a repeat of OpenSea's exit scenario? Why OpenRouter Chooses to "Sell Itself" This isn't the first time Alex Atallah has built a company worth tens of billions of dollars. Previously, this serial entrepreneur co-founded OpenSea, bringing NFTs from a niche community into the mainstream. At the height of the NFT craze, OpenSea grew from a little-known platform into the world's largest NFT marketplace, with a valuation exceeding $13 billion, and the two founders' net worth reaching approximately $2.2 billion. However, before the NFT market cooled significantly in 2022, Alex Atallah chose to leave OpenSea. Subsequently, as the industry bubble burst, OpenSea's valuation shrank dramatically, and the former NFT leader gradually lost its peak glory. Alex Atallah's early departure was seen by the market as a significant peak signal. Afterwards, Alex Atallah turned his attention to AI infrastructure, and his company, OpenRouter, became the largest hub in the AI ​​era. Currently, OpenRouter has integrated over 400 AI models, boasts approximately 10 million users, and processes over 200 trillion tokens monthly. This year, the number of tokens processed through its API has increased approximately tenfold. However, it's worth noting that while its business is growing rapidly, OpenRouter has not opted for an IPO, but rather pursued a potential sale. The reason is that this is a massive but limited-profit channel business. Currently, OpenRouter primarily profits by charging developers a platform service fee when they call its AI models, with a commission rate of approximately 5%-5.5%. Although its annualized AI inference consumption has reached hundreds of millions of dollars, as of April 2026, the company's annualized revenue is estimated at approximately $50 million. In other words, OpenRouter connects a huge AI demand market, but does not fully control the upstream of the value chain. As model capabilities become increasingly standardized, platforms are vulnerable to the rise of open-source models, ecosystem integration with cloud vendors, and price reductions directly from model suppliers, potentially putting continued pressure on profit margins. OpenRouter will find it difficult to tell a story of exponential growth and exceptionally high profits to the capital market. Meanwhile, competition in the AI ​​model aggregation sector is intensifying. For example, Meta's internal AI incubator overseas is developing a scheduling service to rival OpenRouter, aiming to reduce computational costs associated with code development; domestically, large model aggregation platforms such as Cheetah Mobile's EasyRouter and NetEase Youdao's ThinkFlow have emerged. Therefore, the current high valuation given to OpenRouter by the market is more about pricing its future potential than its current profitability. However, for potential buyers, OpenRouter's truly attractive asset may not be its current revenue scale, but rather its long-term accumulation of real-world AI usage data. By connecting hundreds of models and tens of millions of users, OpenRouter has accumulated a vast amount of usage data from real-world production environments, including performance differences between different models in actual tasks, developer preferences, price sensitivity, and the substitutability between open-source and closed-source models. Compared to lab test data, this real-world AI usage data is closer to market demand and more difficult to replicate quickly through short-term investment. This may be a key reason why large tech companies are willing to pay a premium for OpenRouter. From NFTs to AI, Alex Atallah has twice caught the wave of technological advancements. If OpenRouter is ultimately sold for tens of billions of dollars, does it signify a repricing of AI infrastructure value, or is it a signal of another cycle peak? The answer may still require time to prove.

data.title: Convicted Fraudster Linked To Nigel Farage Received $9M Through Polymarket Account From Unidentified Source
data.description:
A Polymarket account reportedly linked to George Cottrell, a convicted fraudster and longtime associate of Reform UK leader Nigel Farage, received nearly $9 million in cryptocurrency from unidentified sources before placing multimillion-dollar bets on Donald Trump's US election victory.

Betting account in George Cottrell’s name received $9mn in crypto from unidentified sources

Great @AnnaSophieGross / FT scoophttps://t.co/jBlQHRoVaY

— Gabriel Pogrund (@Gabriel_Pogrund) July 23, 2026

According to a report by the Financial Times, the account operating under the username "GCottrell93" received approximately $8.8 million in October 2024 through a series of crypto transfers before deploying the funds on prediction market bets tied to the US presidential race.

Roughly $7 million reportedly arrived through five separate transactions from a wallet connected to crypto exchange OKX, while another $1.83 million was transferred from a wallet associated with ChangeNOW.

Interesting piece from the @FT on the money man behind @Nigel_Farage and @reformparty_uk, George Cottrell. Money in, multiple wallets, tumble, bet, mix, tumgle again, dry, and comes out clean as Persil. https://t.co/i3BuT5SRtgpic.twitter.com/q9nS8aWhPm

— The Finance Guy (@OneFinanceGuy) July 23, 2026

The Financial Times said the funds were used almost immediately to wager on Trump winning the election. Following his victory, around $13 million in winnings was reportedly transferred back to an OKX wallet, while approximately $282,000 was sent to ChangeNOW.

Cottrell was convicted of wire fraud in the United States in 2017 after agreeing to launder proceeds from drug trafficking.

Financial scrutiny around Farage ally deepens

The revelation adds to growing scrutiny surrounding Cottrell's financial dealings and his relationship with Nigel Farage, which has come under increased public attention over the past year.

Farage previously faced criticism after it emerged that he had accepted, without declaring, a £5 million gift from billionaire investor and major Tether backer Christopher Harborne.

Left: Nigel Farage says he wasn't in politics at the time George Cottrell was donating to him

Right: George Cottrell literally had business cards with Nigel Farage's email on them pic.twitter.com/ear0PmgBKT

— Farrukh (@implausibleblog) July 8, 2026

Separate reporting also revealed that Cottrell had funded staff, security, accommodation and other expenses for Farage ahead of the Reform UK leader's 2024 election campaign. The undeclared support has since been referred to the UK's Parliamentary Commissioner for Standards.

Cottrell has remained an active Polymarket user throughout the year. Earlier in 2026, he reportedly wagered $41,000 that the conflict involving the United States and Iran would continue for another four months. One of his largest open positions now is a roughly $71,000 bet on US Vice President JD Vance securing the Republican presidential nomination in 2028.

This guy made $2.9M from a single bet on 🇪🇸 Spain to win the World Cup.

He spent weeks building a $446k position on Spain.

Today it paid out:
> bet: $446k
> payout: $3.3M
> profit: +$2.9M (+650%)

He also backed England, Brazil and Belgium, losing just $73.5k combined.

Spain… https://t.co/c01NNTTprjpic.twitter.com/8dkVNLTBjf

— me_cool_off (@me_cool_off) July 20, 2026

Report uncovers fraudulent identity and overlapping financial network

Separate reporting by Byline Times uncovered another layer to Cottrell's financial history, alleging that he previously operated using a fraudulent Swiss passport under the name "Oscar Drewitt."

🔴 NEW ‘Posh George’, Two Passports, and a Fraudulent Identity: Convicted Fraudster and Nigel Farage Funder’s Connections to Reform UK’s Murky Financial Structures

Why did Reform UK change from being a privately-owned company to a non-profit company limited by guarantee in 2025?…

— Byline Times (@BylineTimes) July 23, 2026

According to the report, Reform UK's former treasurer, Mehrtash A'zami, was aware of the alias and hired Cottrell into a City financial network while he was using the false identity. Another Polymarket bettor, Hon Kong Yong, reportedly introduced Cottrell into that network under the same name.

Byline Times further reported that Yong and A'zami have companies registered at the same office in Tivat, Montenegro — an address also linked to Harborne and two other former Reform UK officials.

The combined reports place renewed attention on the source of millions of dollars that flowed through Cottrell's Polymarket account and the broader network of political and financial relationships surrounding one of Farage's closest associates.

data.title: Trump's "pig butchering scam": die-hard supporters go hungry nine times in three days
data.description:
Desperate to revive his fortune, Donald Trump has called on his political supporters to become his business partners. Those who trusted him most have suffered the greatest losses.

Despite the significant setbacks in various Trump-related investment products in the public market, the Trump family has profited handsomely and still holds a large amount of liquid assets. Image source: CHARLES BRUCALIERE FOR FORBES VIA GEMINI

Original title: "Unable to buy shares in OpenAI and Anthropic?" Their related products are currently experiencing a boom in the secondhand market. Vadim Fistikan started working at the age of 17, accumulating savings through years of overtime work, postponing various consumptions, and self-restraint as an adult. By the time he was nearly 30, his savings exceeded $100,000. In 2021, this Washington state truck driver took a liking to a waterfront home in Florida. The house had a swimming pool, a waterway connecting to a river and flowing into the ocean, and his family lived nearby. “We were all set to buy the house,” he said, “but then all sorts of Trump-related investment projects started going viral.” The core target of this frenzy was Trump Media and Technology Group (TMTG), the operating entity of the social media platform Truth Social. In October 2021, Trump officially launched the company's development plan, declaring his intention to create a social media platform without content moderation, and opening investment channels to supporters through a special purpose acquisition company (SPAC). The SPAC's stock price surged 1650% in two days, before falling back about 30% by the opening of the third trading day. But in Fistican's view, this wasn't a risk warning, but rather an opportunity to buy at a discount. “I thought I had to get in.” This voter, who voted for Trump three times, kept increasing his investment, eventually reaching a total of $205,000. Now, that investment is worth only $30,000. Last fall, Fistican logged onto a real-life social media platform to express his dissatisfaction. “I posted that it was a complete scam,” he recalled. “But many netizens argued with me, saying I was a Trump hater. I replied to them: I’ve been a staunch believer in the project since its inception… and now I’m penniless.” He wasn’t the only one who lost money. Katherine Chiles, the former CFO of the SPAC, has now shared a song on her real social media account with lyrics accusing Trump of betraying his supporters, directly calling Donald Trump a “bastard.” Chad Nedohin, a church worship singer who previously acted as a lead figure for retail shareholders in Trump Media, now states that the company was merely a money-making tool for Trump and his inner circle. “In their eyes, we are nothing more than leeks to be harvested for profit,” Nedokhin said. “He doesn’t care about anyone.” Throughout Trump’s life, his iconic ventures—the Atlantic City casinos, the IPO on the NYSE, and the nationwide political movement—all relied on one type of person: his loyal followers. When Trump left office in 2021, he still had a massive number of supporters. He quickly launched a monetization plan, a three-step operation: First, he created business projects from scratch, investing almost no of his own capital but acquiring the majority of equity; second, he encouraged his loyal followers to enter the market frantically, cashing out as much profit as possible; third, he watched the assets collapse and divided up the remaining residual value. Between 2021 and 2025, five Trump family-owned projects raised funds from ordinary investors: Trump Media Technology Group, World Liberty Financial, Trump Memecoin, Melania Trump Memecoin, and American Bitcoin. Now, all of these projects have fallen into the third stage of collapse. Trump Media's stock price has plummeted 89% from its peak; World Freedom Financial Token has fallen 82%; Trump meme coins have shrunk by 98%; Melania meme coins have plunged 99%; and the stock price of U.S. Bitcoin Company has dropped 95%. Forbes estimates that the Trump family has cashed out a total of $1.9 billion, still netting $3.1 billion overall; investors have collectively lost approximately $7 billion. Representatives for the Trump Organization did not respond to requests for comment; a spokesperson for Trump Media also declined to comment. The company has sued Forbes and other media outlets, alleging that reports of losses in its first year of operation were inaccurate. The White House ignored questions about the president's various profit-making business projects, issuing only a policy statement. Deputy Press Secretary Anna Kelly stated, "President Trump deeply cares about the patriotic people who voted for him and works tirelessly every day to advocate for their rights." The painful cost of this scam to ordinary people is evident in the experience of Fistickan. He wasn't just buying stocks; he was also buying a whole set of elaborate rhetoric—claiming that Trump would treat his supporters as partners, not tools for exploitation. “I bet almost my entire life savings on this one stock,” Fistican said. “It was the worst robbery and scam he’d ever pulled.” In January 2025, Nick Pinto was dining at the Champion restaurant at Trump’s Miami golf resort, decorated with solid wood and leather, when his phone received a Robinhood push notification: Trump was launching his own meme currency. The 26-year-old Pinto primarily earns a living by creating videos for various social media platforms. His due diligence consisted of simply searching on Google and browsing related content on Twitter. “I thought, I’ll buy some and see how it goes,” he said, initially investing $7,000. Pinto was one of the ordinary investors caught up in this investment frenzy. This craze brought enormous wealth to the "First Family" as Trump returned to the White House. The source of all this was World Freedom Finance, a cryptocurrency project launched by Trump the previous September. Justin Sun, the billionaire embroiled in SEC charges, invested $45 million in the project's tokens after Trump's election victory, and later added another $100 million to purchase Trump's personal meme token. Melania Trump also launched her own meme token. Around the time Trump officially entered the White House, these three tokens traded extremely well, generating hundreds of millions of dollars in cash-out profits for the Trump family. Trump then offered an additional perk: the top 220 memecoin investors by holding size would be able to attend a dinner at his private golf club in Virginia. "I was completely blinded by greed," Pinto said. He invested an additional $480,000, nearly 60% of his entire investment portfolio in the memecoin, enough to secure his place at the dinner. The dinner was both absurd and shabby. Pinto said it felt like a wedding, with mediocre food and poor service. “They didn’t even provide me with soda; I only drank water the whole time.” Another attendee recalled offering former NBA player Lamar Odom a cigarette. While they were standing outside, Odom took a video call. He said to Khloe Kardashian on the other end, “I’m with these crypto-trading young people!” After the dinner, the price of this memecoin plummeted and never recovered, losing two-thirds of its value by the end of the year. Around this time, Pinto, persuaded by a friend who also attended the dinner, turned against the token. Pinto relayed his friend's words: "This thing was launched out of nowhere by the Trump family. Nobody can predict the future trend of this token, nor do they know what their plans are for it." Pinto was persuaded and sold 75% of his holdings, locking in a loss of approximately $250,000. He kept the remaining position, thinking that a major turnaround might occur later. And indeed, a turnaround did come. In April of the same year, Trump held another investor event at Mar-a-Lago, where guests received a gift bag containing Trump collectible cards, a presidential life-size statue with cologne, and a Trump-branded watch. “I almost didn’t get a single gift bag; someone kept stealing mine,” Pinto recalled. One attendee secretly took six. “After my gift bag was stolen, I saw one on an empty chair, so I grabbed one too.” Trump hosted two offline events for meme holders, one at his golf course in Virginia and the other at Mar-a-Lago in Palm Beach. Attendees were able to leave with a bag full of Trump-branded merchandise. At an event at Mar-a-Lago in April, Trump's business partner Bill Zanker said, "I'm persuading the president to hold one of these events every six months." The gift set includes a cologne bottle featuring a gold portrait of Trump, priced at $249 online. Nick Pinto attended both meme coin events, saying, "I haven't used it yet; I'm just using it as a decorative piece." The red-dial watch became a sought-after gift at the Mar-a-Lago event. Trump's online store also sells other watch models, one of which incorporates fabric fragments taken from the suit and tie he wore when his headshot was taken at the Fulton County Jail in Georgia in 2023 after he was indicted on multiple charges, including organized racketeering.

Portrait Collector's Cards

This set of Trump trading cards features his slogan after being attacked in Butler, Pennsylvania, where a bullet grazed his ear: "Fight to the end! Fight to the end! Fight to the end!"

Dark Night Limited Baseball Cap

Traditional red "Make America Great Again (MAGA)" baseball caps are long gone. Guests attending this meme-coin themed dinner each received a black cap printed with Trump's portrait, a replica of his signature, and the meme coin name $TRUMP. "I thought to myself, 'Let's buy some and see how it goes; nobody can predict what will happen next.'" Some attendees wanted much more than just cheap Trump souvenirs. Before the first dinner, Huma Finance co-founder Erbil Karaman made an additional investment; at the time, the White House was reviewing stablecoin legislation, and these dollar-pegged cryptocurrencies were the company's tool for cross-border fund transfers. Karaman stated that he did not engage in any lobbying activities at the event. Two months later, he received an invitation from the White House to attend the signing ceremony of the relevant bill. Those who truly made a fortune from Trump memes were mostly the people who least cared about the token. Morten Christensen, founder of the crypto news website airdropalert.com, bought the token early on, and decisively sold it off a few days after the market hype subsided, making a profit of nearly 30 times his initial investment. To secure his place at both dinners, he simultaneously held the token and opened short positions to hedge; he spent approximately $1,200 in fees to secure a seat at the first event, and only $500 for the second. “Those die-hard MAGA supporters wholeheartedly agree with this narrative,” Christensen said. “I’m here purely to make money. I’ve joined several chat groups of these supporters, and they always ignore all the facts and data… I almost feel sorry for them.” Even Justin Sun has stopped pretending that Trump’s crypto products have investment value. He filed a lawsuit in April, accusing World Freedom Finance of coercing him to make additional investments and freezing his token assets after he refused to cooperate. Sun accused the company of fraud and extortion, while World Freedom Finance argued that it had the right to freeze Sun’s tokens and countersued him for defamation. Sun also claimed that the company began borrowing heavily in high-risk loans after channeling huge sums of money to Trump and other insiders. Besides Trump himself, he was the one who most enthusiastically promoted the project to the public, but now he bluntly states that the project is “on the verge of collapse.” Capitol Hill Riots Fuel Fundraising Scams (January 6, 2021) Trump supporters stormed the U.S. Capitol. Twitter permanently banned Trump's personal account, providing him with marketing material; Amazon stopped providing cloud server hosting services for the right-wing social media platform Parler, creating a supply gap in the market. January 27, 2021 A week after leaving office, Trump met with two former contestants of "The Apprentice" at Mar-a-Lago to discuss building a media technology empire. October 20, 2021 Trump announced Trump Media & Technology, a social media company planning to build an online ecosystem aligned with Trump's vision, with its first product being a Twitter-like, real-world social media platform. October 22, 2021: Digital World, a special purpose acquisition company, planned to acquire Trump Media to go public. The stock surged to an all-time high of $175, valuing the company, which had barely conducted any substantial business, at over $25 billion. March 25, 2024: After years of regulatory hurdles, Trump Media finally merged with Digital World, officially becoming a publicly traded company. The stock jumped 59% the day after the merger. On September 30, 2024, during the final stretch of the presidential election, Trump announced a new project on social media: "I promised to make America great again, and this time we're delivering on that promise with cryptocurrency. A free world of finance will help America become the global crypto capital!" However, the project has received a consistently lukewarm market response. On November 5, 2024, Donald Trump defeated Kamala Harris, becoming the first U.S. president to be elected twice without consecutive terms since Grover Cleveland in 1892. November 25, 2024: Justin Sun, a cryptocurrency billionaire embroiled in charges by the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, purchased $30 million worth of World Freedom Financial Tokens, boosting the project's popularity. The project ultimately generated over $800 million in revenue for Trump. January 17, 2025: Three days before his inauguration, Trump launched his personal meme coin. The fine print in the agreement warned buyers that the token was not an investment, but Trump supporters still flocked to it, creating a trading frenzy and ultimately generating over $600 million in revenue for him. January 19, 2025: Melania Trump launched her own meme coin to boost her personal wealth. Financial disclosures show that the token, which has plummeted 99% from its all-time high, brought the First Lady $6 million. May 12, 2025: A US-based Bitcoin company announced plans to merge with a special purpose acquisition company, causing investors to buy the stock, driving it up 448%; subsequently, the stock has fallen 96%. As doubts arose among his supporters, the Trump family began a series of intriguing asset adjustments. The self-proclaimed "World Free Finance Project," capable of disrupting the traditional financial system, generated approximately $850 million in revenue for Trump. Subsequently, Trump heavily invested in the most conventional and stable assets in the financial market—municipal bonds. Meanwhile, Trump Media Group, rather than being a social media company, became a major holder of Bitcoin. When the company's stock price was high, it raised $2.4 billion through equity and debt financing, and then invested all of this capital in Bitcoin as its price approached its historical high. This operation resulted in the loss of approximately $1 billion in shareholder equity, but left the company with an asset with a far longer lifespan than real social media. This supposedly "free speech" social media platform recently banned a Forbes reporter who used the platform to contact shareholders. The Trump Media Group claims to empower everyone with a voice, but its definition of who has that voice seems remarkably narrow. During Trump's presidency, his eldest son, Donald Trump Jr., and younger son, Eric Trump, managed the day-to-day operations of their father's various assets, replicating his entire profit-making strategy. In March 2025, the brothers partnered with a data center company, acquiring a 20% stake in its Bitcoin mining equipment and renaming the business "Bitcoin USA." What did this data center company gain in return? A group of investors caught up in the hype surrounding Trump. The company could then sell a large number of shares to these investors, with the proceeds more than offsetting the cost of relinquishing the 20% stake. On May 12, 2025, U.S. Bitcoin Company announced its SPAC listing, causing the SPAC's stock price to surge by 448%. Ryan Kenney, 41, a military dependent and former architecture major, now spends most of his time at home caring for his two children. A week after the deal was announced, U.S. Bitcoin Company's stock price fell, and he immediately invested $60,000 in the stock, believing he had capitalized on the low price. However, the stock's value relied entirely on market hype and lacked solid fundamentals, making the widely circulated hype in the meme stock community—"Buy low!" "Buy more as it falls to average down your cost!"—extremely dangerous. Kenney continued to add to his position, eventually investing a total of $98,000, which is now worth only $9,000. Recently, he's been thinking a lot about investors in the same predicament as him, and the overall scale of their losses. "Maybe no one has lost as much as I have," he said, "but there are probably hundreds of thousands of people across the country, each losing $5,000." Returning to Fistican, he still can't let go of his Florida house. With his savings depleted, he had to settle for a less desirable option: a weed-covered plot of land for $18,000, hoping to build a house there someday. He works in freight transport in the Pacific Northwest, and the Iran conflict has driven diesel prices to over $6 a gallon, significantly reducing his income. "The money I make from freight transport now is about the same as what I make making hamburgers at a fast-food restaurant." Recently, facing financial difficulties, Fistican had to sell assets to make ends meet. He could have sold off his entire stake in Trump Media—a company that consistently loses hundreds of millions of dollars annually, yet its market capitalization has long remained more than three times the value of the underlying Bitcoin assets he holds, indicating a severely overvalued stock. However, the stock price movements of these Trump-related stocks are not driven by discounted cash flow valuation logic; rather, they are supported by the loyalty of supporters and the speculative mentality that "the market will rebound when the next hype hits." So Fistican sold his Ford F-150 pickup truck. This is a microcosm of investing in Trump-related stocks. Fistican had hoped to buy a waterfront home in Florida, but now he's left with only a vacant, overgrown lot, a badly lost investment account, and barely enough cash to make ends meet.

data.title: Stripe's ambition: to build the financial infrastructure for the era of AI and stablecoins.
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Jinse Finance

Summary: Stripe has long been considered one of the world's largest internet payment companies. Recently, Stripe's development direction has changed significantly. From acquiring Bridge and Privy, to rumors of acquiring OpenRouter and PayPal, and jointly promoting Open USD, Stripe is evolving from a payment company into a global financial empire spanning payments, stablecoins, wallets, and AI. What exactly does Stripe want?

I. Negotiating the Acquisition of OpenRouter—Entering the AI ​​Market

Recently, Stripe has made frequent new strategic moves, launching a series of initiatives.

According to the Wall Street Journal, citing sources familiar with the matter, Stripe is in talks to acquire AI model aggregation platform OpenRouter, and the deal could be finalized soon. While negotiations are not yet finalized, the potential transaction amount is approximately $10 billion, while OpenRouter's valuation after its latest funding round in May was approximately $1.3 billion. This means that if the acquisition is successful, its valuation will jump nearly eightfold in a short period.

If this acquisition is successful, it will signify Stripe's official expansion from the payment infrastructure sector into AI infrastructure. Unlike large AI models like OpenAI and Anthropic, OpenRouter itself does not train large models; instead, it acts as a model switch for the AI ​​era. Currently, OpenRouter has aggregated hundreds of large language models, including those from OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Meta, and numerous open-source models. Developers only need to access a single API provided by OpenRouter to freely switch between different models without needing to interface with each model vendor separately. According to Stripe's official website, the two companies reached a partnership as early as the end of January this year: the OpenRouter platform has already utilized Stripe to support its revenue growth, global expansion, and fraud prevention. Stripe's collaboration with OpenRouter allows developers to route model requests through OpenRouter, while Stripe automatically tracks usage, applies appropriate pricing, and handles billing. The collaboration between Stripe and OpenRouter enables developers to automatically respond to cost changes and scale profitability without increasing operational complexity. Additionally, it's worth noting the founder of the OpenRouter platform—Alex Atallah. Alex Atallah co-founded the NFT trading platform OpenSea with Devin Finzer in 2017, serving as its CTO. During the NFT bull market, OpenSea grew into the world's largest NFT trading platform, with monthly transaction volume exceeding $4 billion between 2017 and 2022, making it one of the most successful startups in Web3 history. In 2022, Alex Atallah left OpenSea's management but retained a board seat, subsequently founding OpenRouter in 2023, officially shifting its focus to the AI ​​infrastructure sector. II. Plans to Jointly Acquire PayPal—Completing the Payment Landscape As early as the end of February this year, Stripe expressed initial interest in acquiring all or part of PayPal's business, but a deal has yet to be reached. On July 15, according to two sources familiar with the matter, payment company Stripe and private equity firm Advent International jointly offered to acquire PayPal (PYPL.O) for $60.50 per share, a deal that would value the payment company at over $53 billion. The sources said the offer, submitted earlier this month, has secured approximately $50 billion in committed financing from banks, representing a premium of about 28% over PayPal's closing price on Tuesday. Another source revealed that PayPal's board believes the $53 billion acquisition offer from rival Stripe and Advent International undervalues ​​the company and faces regulatory and financing difficulties. Stripe is a payment infrastructure provider, while PayPal is more consumer-oriented.
